_purpose: Seasonal calendar for the TX East Piney Woods cluster. Month-by-month agricultural, social, economic, and weather patterns across three eras — cotton agriculture (1830-1900), lumber and mixed agriculture (1880-1930), and oil field era (1930-1960). East Texas had a nine-plus-month growing season, but the cotton calendar ruled the year from planting in March through picking in September-November. Pine logging was a winter occupation — the sap ran down and the logs moved easier. The oil field ran year-round and erased the seasonal calendar entirely.
display_name: TX East Piney Woods — Seasonal Calendar Profile
primary_economy: Cotton agriculture (1830-1900); pine timber and cotton (1880-1930); oil and diversified agriculture (1930-1960); rose cultivation in Smith County (1890-1960)

calendar_eras.description: The cotton calendar governed East Texas life for seventy years. East Texas had a long growing season — nine months or more — and mild winters that allowed earlier planting and later picking than the Upper South. Cotton was the cash crop; corn, sweet potatoes, peas, and hogs were the subsistence base. The Harrison County plantation economy ran on enslaved labor and produced cotton on a commercial scale shipped by steamboat. The yeoman counties (Nacogdoches, Angelina, Cherokee, Smith, Rusk, Cass) produced cotton on a smaller scale, supplemented by subsistence farming and open-range livestock. The lay-by period in midsummer was the one break the crop allowed, and it coincided with the revival season and the worst of the summer heat.

january.agricultural: Fields dormant. Fence repair, firewood cutting, hog killing (late December through January). Preparing plow gear. Clearing new ground — East Texas farmers continuously expanded their cultivated acreage by girdling and burning pine trees. In Harrison County, enslaved workers maintained ditches, split rails, and cleared land.
january.social_civic: Church services on Sunday (Baptist, Methodist, Cumberland Presbyterian). Court day at the county seat. Visiting among neighbors limited by cold and mud. In Harrison County, the enslaved population had a brief respite from field work.
january.economic: The previous fall's cotton was still being transported to market — by steamboat from Jefferson or by wagon to the nearest gin. The crop-lien cycle for the new year began at the crossroads store. Credit extended against next fall's crop at interest rates exceeding 25%.
january.weather_conditions: Cool to cold — average 40-50F. Hard freezes possible but rare in the southern counties. Rain common. Sandy roads passable; red clay stretches treacherous.
january.narrative_hook: January in {county} County was when {ancestor_name} counted what was left from the fall cotton and calculated whether the family could reach planting season without going deeper to the merchant — and in East Texas, the answer was usually deeper.

february.agricultural: Late winter fieldwork began. Breaking ground for spring planting on warm days. Burning brush piles from winter clearing. Garden preparation near the house. In the pine woods, winter logging operations continued — February was prime logging season with sap down and ground relatively firm.
february.social_civic: Limited social activity. Church services. Quilting bees and corn shuckings in some communities.
february.economic: Credit extended at the store. Seed purchases for spring planting. Logging operations provided the only significant winter cash income outside the plantation economy.
february.weather_conditions: Warming — 45-55F. Late-season cold snaps possible. Rain increases. The red clay subsoil saturated easily.
february.narrative_hook: February in the pine woods was logging season — {ancestor_name} could earn cash wages cutting timber while the fields were dormant, the sap was down, and the logs slid easier on the cool ground.

march.agricultural: Plowing began in earnest. Cotton planting preparation — beds laid up, furrows opened. East Texas's long growing season meant planting could begin in late March, two to three weeks earlier than Alabama or Mississippi. Corn planted on the earliest warm days. Garden plots started — beans, squash, sweet potatoes.
march.social_civic: Easter observances (when Easter fell in March). Spring court session. Community gathered for planting.
march.economic: Seed and supply purchases at peak. Crop-lien merchants did maximum credit business. Logging operations winding down as sap rose.
march.weather_conditions: Warm — 55-65F. Dogwood and redbud blooming in the understory. Spring thunderstorms began — heavy enough to delay plowing on clay soils.
march.narrative_hook: March in {county} County was when {ancestor_name} put the mule to the sandy loam and started the year's bet — cotton seed in the ground, credit drawn at the store, and nine months until the verdict came in at the gin.

april.agricultural: Cotton planting. Seeds dropped in furrows by hand. Corn already up. Garden vegetables planted. The East Texas growing season was long enough to support two plantings of some garden crops. Rose nurseries in Smith County were at peak propagation season — budding and grafting rootstock.
april.social_civic: Easter services (most years). Circuit rider visits to outlying congregations. Community fish fries at creek crossings.
april.economic: Full credit extension. Farmers committed to the crop — seed in the ground, debt accumulating.
april.weather_conditions: Warm and humid — 62-72F. Thunderstorms frequent. Tornadoes possible across the pine belt.
april.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} dropped cotton seed in the East Texas sand in April {year}, the growing season stretched nine months ahead — longer than Alabama, longer than Mississippi, and every extra day was a day the crop could make or the debt could grow.

may.agricultural: Cotton chopping — hoeing between rows to control weeds. First cultivation with plow. Corn growing fast in the warm soil. Garden producing early vegetables — squash, beans, greens.
may.social_civic: Church services. Road maintenance (county corvee labor). Community work events.
may.economic: No income. Store credit accumulating. The lean season began.
may.weather_conditions: Hot — 72-82F. Humidity rising. Thunderstorms common. The pine woods held moisture — shade kept the forest cool but the cleared fields baked.
may.narrative_hook: May was chopping season — {ancestor_name} with a hoe between the cotton rows, fighting weeds in sandy soil that grew them as fast as cotton, while the pine woods at the field edge stood dark and cool and unreachable.

june.agricultural: Cotton growing. Second cultivation. Corn tasseling. Sweet potatoes vining. Peach harvest in the orchards planted on the old Cherokee clearings.
june.social_civic: Social life expanding as days lengthened. Saturday gatherings at crossroads stores.
june.economic: No income. Credit deepening. Watermelons from the garden provided one of the few pleasures of the lean season.
june.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 80-90F. East Texas humidity was brutal — the pine forest trapped moisture and the cleared fields radiated heat. Afternoon thunderstorms offered brief relief.
june.narrative_hook: June in East Texas was when the heat and the humidity settled in and would not leave until October — {ancestor_name} working the cotton in air thick enough to drink, the pine woods shimmering at the field edge.

july.agricultural: Lay-by time — cotton growing on its own, little cultivation possible. The brief respite in the agricultural calendar. Some families used lay-by to fish, hunt, visit kin, or attend to deferred tasks. In the timber regions, summer was dead season for logging — sap was up, insects were thick, and the heat made heavy labor dangerous.
july.social_civic: Revival season began in late July. Camp meetings in the pine groves drew families from across the county for multiday religious services combining worship, socializing, and courtship. Fourth of July celebrations in the county seats.
july.economic: No income. Store credit at maximum. Odd jobs for cash — rail splitting, well digging — during lay-by.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 82-95F. Drought risk. The pine woods provided shade but the humidity made the heat oppressive. Mosquitoes at peak — malaria was endemic in East Texas through the early twentieth century.
july.narrative_hook: Lay-by in July was when {ancestor_name} could stop hoeing and wait for the cotton to make itself — time for the revival meeting in the pine grove and visiting kin, while the East Texas heat hung thick enough to see and the mosquitoes sang in the twilight.

august.agricultural: Cotton bolls forming and beginning to open. Early varieties ready for first picking. Corn maturing. Sweet potatoes ready. Fodder pulling — stripping corn leaves for winter animal feed.
august.social_civic: Revival season at peak. Baptist and Methodist week-long revivals — the defining social and spiritual event of the East Texas year. Baptisms in creeks and rivers followed the revival. Courtship season for young people.
august.economic: Anticipation of harvest income. Merchants calculating expected returns against credit extended.
august.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 82-95F. Occasional drought stress on cotton. Hurricane remnants from the Gulf could bring heavy rain that damaged open bolls.
august.narrative_hook: August revival in {county} County was when the preacher's voice replaced the chopping rhythm — {ancestor_name} in the pine-shaded arbor for a week of evening services, singing and praying and deciding things the cotton field could not decide.

september.agricultural: Cotton picking began in earnest. The entire family in the field — men, women, children. On Harrison County plantations, enslaved workers picked from dawn to dusk under overseer supervision. Cotton hauled to the gin by wagon. The nine-month growing season meant East Texas cotton matured later than Alabama cotton, extending the picking season into November.
september.social_civic: Picking consumed all social time. Church attendance dropped as harvest demanded every hand.
september.economic: First income of the year as cotton reached the gin. The crop-lien debt settled against proceeds. East Texas yields varied widely — Harrison County plantation cotton on rich soil averaged better yields than the sandy upland cotton of Nacogdoches or Angelina.
september.weather_conditions: Moderating — 75-85F. First cool mornings by late September. Hurricane risk from the Gulf Coast — a major storm could flatten standing cotton.
september.narrative_hook: September was the reckoning — {ancestor_name} in the cotton field from first light to dark, every boll picked by hand, knowing the count at the gin would either clear the store debt or leave the family deeper in the hole.

october.agricultural: Cotton picking at peak. Corn harvested and stored. Sweet potatoes dug. Hogs turned into the woods to fatten on mast (acorns from the post oaks and hickory).
october.social_civic: County court session. County fairs in the larger towns. Tyler's rose culture celebrations began in this era.
october.economic: Primary income month. Cotton ginned and sold. The year's economic fate determined at the gin scale.
october.weather_conditions: Cool and pleasant — 58-68F. First frost by late October in the northern counties. The one truly comfortable month in the East Texas calendar.
october.narrative_hook: October was when {ancestor_name} stood at the gin in {county} County and learned whether the year had won or lost — the cotton weighed, the price set, and the merchant waiting with the ledger.

november.agricultural: Late cotton picking. Fields cleaned. Winter preparation — firewood cutting, fence repair. Hog killing began in late November. Pine logging season opened as sap dropped and temperatures cooled.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving observances. Hunting season — deer, turkey, squirrel, possum. East Texas's dense pine and hardwood forest supported abundant game.
november.economic: Final cotton sales. Debt settlement with the merchant. Logging operations offering cash wages attracted farm families supplementing cotton income.
november.weather_conditions: Cool — 48-58F. First hard frost. The pine woods turned from green to a mosaic of pine green and hardwood gold and red.
november.narrative_hook: By November {year}, {ancestor_name} knew whether the year had paid or not — the cotton money counted against the store debt, and the answer determined whether the family entered Christmas free or already owing for next year's crop.

december.agricultural: Hog killing — the central food event of the year. Meat cured in salt and smoke. Fields dormant. Pine logging operations running — December through February was prime logging season. Firewood cutting and storage.
december.social_civic: Christmas — the paramount social event. Church services, family visiting, modest celebrations. On Harrison County plantations, enslaved workers typically received a brief respite from field work and small provisions. Christmas was the one season when the boundaries between work and life relaxed.
december.economic: Year-end accounting. Families who cleared their debt had a brief respite. Families who did not entered January deeper in the cycle.
december.weather_conditions: Cool to cold — 42-52F. Occasional freezes. Rain common. The pine woods were gray and still, the deciduous understory bare.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas in {county} County was when {ancestor_name} could stop for a week — the hogs killed and hung, the cotton gone to market, and the pine woods quiet enough to hear the church bell from the next farm over.

calendar_eras.description: The lumber industry overlaid the cotton calendar with a counter-seasonal rhythm. Pine logging was a winter operation — October through March, when the sap was down, the insects were dormant, and the cooler temperatures made heavy labor possible. The sawmills ran year-round, but log supply peaked in winter. This created a dual calendar: farmers who also logged worked cotton in the spring-summer-fall and timber in the winter, rarely idle. The lumber camps had their own calendar — the work was steady, the whistle replaced the sunrise, and the seasons mattered only as they affected the logs and the roads.

january.agricultural: Fields dormant. Subsistence tasks. Farm families who also logged were in the timber camps.
january.lumber_industry: Peak logging season. Crews in the pine forest from dawn to dusk felling trees, bucking logs, and hauling to the tramway siding. Sawmills running at full capacity processing winter-cut logs. Cold air kept the resin down and the insects out.
january.social_civic: Lumber camp life — commissary, bunkhouse, Saturday night gatherings. Farm communities quiet.
january.economic: Logging wages provided the only significant winter cash income. Sawmill workers earned $1.00-$1.50/day; loggers earned similar or slightly more for the heavier work.
january.weather_conditions: Cool — 40-50F. Ideal logging weather — cool enough for heavy work, dry enough for log roads.
january.narrative_hook: January in the pine woods was logging season — {ancestor_name} in the timber from first light, dropping longleaf pines that had grown for a hundred years, because the lumber company paid cash and the cotton did not.

february.agricultural: Late winter field preparation beginning on warm days.
february.lumber_industry: Logging continues at peak. Spring planting would pull some farm-loggers back to their fields.
february.social_civic: Limited social activity in both farm and lumber camp communities.
february.economic: Logging wages steady. Seed purchases beginning for spring cotton planting.
february.weather_conditions: Warming — 45-55F. Late cold snaps possible. Mud season beginning on dirt roads.
february.narrative_hook: February was when {ancestor_name} had to choose — keep logging for cash or go back to the cotton field for credit. The lumber company paid weekly. The cotton paid once a year, in October, minus what the store took.

march.agricultural: Cotton planting preparation. Farm-loggers returning from the timber camps to plow.
march.lumber_industry: Logging season winding down as sap rises. Sawmills shift to processing winter-cut inventory. Tramway maintenance season.
march.social_civic: Easter observances. Community transitioning from winter isolation to spring activity.
march.economic: Shift from logging wages back to crop-lien credit for farm families. A critical economic transition each spring.
march.weather_conditions: Warm — 55-65F. Spring rains heavy. Logging roads becoming impassable.
march.narrative_hook: March was the hinge — {ancestor_name} left the logging camp and walked back to the farm, trading the lumber company's cash wages for the cotton merchant's credit book, and the difference was the difference between paid and owing.

april.agricultural: Cotton planting. Corn planting. Garden crops in. Rose nurseries at peak propagation.
april.lumber_industry: Sawmills running on winter-cut inventory. No active logging — too warm, too wet, sap running.
april.social_civic: Church services. Community fish fries. Tyler area: rose cultivation activities at peak.
april.economic: Full credit extension at the store. Lumber company seasonal layoffs for some workers.
april.narrative_hook: April in Tyler was when the roses bloomed and the cotton went in — {ancestor_name} in a county where the beauty of the nursery fields ran alongside the hard arithmetic of the cotton patch.

may.lumber_industry: Sawmills running. Minimum logging crew for local salvage and deadfall.
may.social_civic: Road work. Church. The pace of life accelerating toward summer.
may.economic: No farm income. Sawmill wages steady for year-round employees.
may.narrative_hook: May separated the worlds — {ancestor_name} in the cotton field, hearing the sawmill whistle from town, knowing one economy paid every week and the other paid once a year.

june.agricultural: Cotton growing. Second cultivation. Garden production.
june.lumber_industry: Sawmills at reduced output as winter log inventory depleted. Millpond levels maintained.
june.social_civic: Social season expanding. Fishing in the creeks and rivers.
june.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 80-90F. East Texas summer fully established.
june.narrative_hook: June was when the East Texas heat became a physical presence — {ancestor_name} working in humidity that left sweat standing on the skin from dawn, the pine woods dark and motionless at the field's edge.

july.agricultural: Lay-by for cotton. Garden at peak production — watermelons, peas, okra.
july.lumber_industry: Sawmills at summer maintenance. Logging crews idle or on minimal work.
july.social_civic: Revival season. Camp meetings. Fourth of July. The social peak of the East Texas year.
july.economic: Maximum credit extension. Odd jobs for cash during lay-by.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest — 82-95F. Mosquitoes, heat exhaustion risk, malaria endemic.
july.narrative_hook: July lay-by was when the camp meeting arbor in the pine grove became the center of {county} County — {ancestor_name} under the brush arbor with the whole community, hearing the preacher and the singing and feeling something the cotton field never provided.

august.agricultural: Early cotton opening. Harvest preparation. Fodder pulling.
august.lumber_industry: Sawmills resuming fuller operations. Logging crews preparing for fall season start.
august.social_civic: Revival peak. Baptist immersion baptisms in creeks and rivers.
august.narrative_hook: August revival and the first cotton bolls opening — {ancestor_name} in a county where the spiritual harvest and the agricultural harvest arrived in the same month.

september.agricultural: Cotton picking. All hands in the field.
september.lumber_industry: Fall logging season beginning. Cooler weather enabled heavier work. Sap beginning to drop in the pines.
september.economic: First cotton income. Logging wages resuming for farm-loggers.
september.narrative_hook: September was when {ancestor_name} worked two calendars — cotton in the morning, timber in the afternoon, sleeping in between.

october.agricultural: Cotton picking at peak. Corn harvest. Sweet potatoes. Hog fattening.
october.lumber_industry: Logging season fully underway. Crews in the pine forest. Tramways running loaded cars to the sawmill.
october.social_civic: County fairs. Court sessions. Tyler rose harvest — the roses that would ship across the nation.
october.economic: Cotton income and logging income overlapping — the best month of the year for families working both.
october.weather_conditions: Cool and pleasant — 58-68F. First frost approaching.
october.narrative_hook: October was the one month {ancestor_name} had money from two directions — cotton at the gin and logging wages from the timber company — and both at once felt like wealth in a county where one alone barely covered the bills.

november.agricultural: Late picking. Hog killing. Winter crop storage.
november.lumber_industry: Peak logging season approaching. Full crews. Long days in the timber.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving. Hunting season — deer, turkey, squirrel.
november.economic: Final cotton settlement. Logging wages steady.
november.narrative_hook: November was hog killing and timber falling — {ancestor_name} curing meat for the winter and earning cash wages from the pine forest at the same time, because East Texas demanded both.

december.lumber_industry: Peak logging season. Christmas shutdown of 2-3 days at most lumber camps; some operated through.
december.social_civic: Christmas — family visiting, church services, modest celebrations.
december.economic: Year-end. Logging wages provided Christmas cash that the cotton economy never could.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas in the lumber camp was short — {ancestor_name} got two days if the company was generous, three if the weather made the log roads impassable, and then the whistle called the crew back to the pines.

calendar_eras.description: The East Texas Oil Field erased the seasonal calendar. Oil wells produced year-round. Drilling rigs operated in all weather. Roughnecks, roustabouts, and pipeline workers worked shifts — 8 or 12 hours, day or night, regardless of season. The oil field calendar had no lay-by, no planting, no harvest. The only breaks were equipment failures, Railroad Commission proration shutdowns, and the rare holiday. For farm families who transitioned to oil field work, the loss of the seasonal rhythm was as disorienting as the gain of steady cash wages was liberating. Tyler maintained its rose cultivation calendar alongside the oil economy, and some farm families continued cotton on the side, creating a triple calendar of oil-cotton-roses unique in the South.

january.oil_field: Full production. Drilling and pumping continued year-round. Cold weather made outdoor pipe work dangerous — frozen fingers on cold steel. Tank cars loaded on railroad sidings regardless of temperature.
january.agricultural_remnant: Subsistence farming continued for families not fully absorbed by the oil economy. Fields dormant. Some cotton allotment farming persisted.
january.social_civic: Oil field boomtown life — saloons, boarding houses, churches established in the boom's wake. Tyler maintained its institutional life separately from the oil field chaos.
january.economic: Steady oil field wages — $4-$6/day for roughnecks, $2-$3/day for roustabouts — dwarfed anything the farm or lumber economy had ever provided.
january.weather_conditions: Cool — 40-50F. Norther cold fronts could drop temperatures 30 degrees in hours. Mud in the oil fields was continuous from October through March.
january.narrative_hook: January in the oil field meant cold steel, frozen mud, and a paycheck — {ancestor_name} earning more in a week than a cotton crop had paid all year.

february.oil_field: Continuous production. Pipeline construction and repair. New wells being spudded (started) as leases were acquired.
february.agricultural_remnant: Farm preparation beginning for families still working land.
february.social_civic: Boomtown conditions: housing shortage, tent cities, improvised schools.
february.economic: Steady wages. The Depression was happening everywhere except the East Texas Oil Field.
february.narrative_hook: In February 1932, the rest of Texas was starving and the East Texas Oil Field was paying cash — {ancestor_name} was in the one place in the state where the Depression had not arrived.

march.oil_field: Full drilling and production. Spring meant improved road conditions and increased activity.
march.agricultural_remnant: Cotton planting for families working both oil and land.
march.social_civic: Oil field towns stabilizing — permanent buildings replacing tents, churches organizing, schools opening.
march.economic: Peak activity month as weather improved.
march.narrative_hook: March in the oil field was when the mud dried enough for the trucks to move without chains — and {ancestor_name} could drive to the rig without spending half the morning digging out.

april.oil_field: Full production. Warm weather improved working conditions but increased fire risk from gas accumulation.
april.agricultural_remnant: Cotton planting alongside oil field work. Rose nurseries at peak propagation. Tyler: the rose industry and oil economy ran in parallel.
april.social_civic: Easter. Community life settling into patterns as the boom matured.
april.economic: Steady to strong. Oil prices fluctuating under Railroad Commission proration orders.
april.weather_conditions: Warm — 62-72F. Thunderstorms created gas ignition risk.
april.narrative_hook: In Tyler, {ancestor_name} lived in a county where the rose nurseries and the oil derricks shared the same sandy soil — beauty and industry side by side, both paying cash.

may.oil_field: Full production. Summer heat beginning to affect outdoor workers.
may.social_civic: Schools out. Children of oil field workers now available for odd jobs.
may.narrative_hook: May on the drilling platform was when {ancestor_name} started counting the hours by how much water {pronoun_subject} drank — the East Texas heat and the rig's steel combined to make every shift a test of endurance.

june.oil_field: Full production. Heat and humidity made outdoor work dangerous. Gas well fires more common in hot weather.
june.social_civic: Long days. Oil field family life establishing routines.
june.economic: Steady wages. Summer expenses lower — no heating costs.
june.narrative_hook: June in the East Texas Oil Field — {ancestor_name} working a twelve-hour shift in air thick with humidity and the smell of crude oil, the derrick shimmering in the heat.

july.oil_field: Full production. No lay-by for the oil field — the wells produced whether the thermometer read 95 or 35.
july.agricultural_remnant: Lay-by for cotton families. Revival season for the church-going.
july.social_civic: Fourth of July. Revival season. Oil field towns had their own social calendar now — movie houses, dance halls, baseball.
july.economic: Steady wages. No shutdown. The oil field's year-round income was its greatest economic difference from the farm.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest — 82-95F. Mosquitoes. Heat exhaustion risk on the rig floor.
july.narrative_hook: The oil field had no lay-by — {ancestor_name} worked July the same as January, the wells producing in the heat the way they produced in the cold, and the paycheck arriving regardless of what the cotton was doing.

august.oil_field: Full production. Pipeline maintenance. Hottest month for outdoor workers.
august.social_civic: Revival meetings continued even in oil field towns — the churches had followed the workers.
august.weather_conditions: Hot — 82-95F. Gulf hurricane risk affecting pipeline operations.
august.narrative_hook: August revival in the oil field town was where two calendars met — {ancestor_name} hearing the same Baptist preacher {pronoun_possessive} grandparents had heard, but walking home to a paycheck from the rig instead of from the cotton gin.

september.oil_field: Full production. Cooling temperatures improved working conditions.
september.social_civic: Schools reopened. Oil field communities stabilizing.
september.economic: Strong month — good working conditions, steady production.
september.narrative_hook: September in {county} County: {ancestor_name} could look one direction and see the cotton being picked the same way it had been for a hundred years, and look the other direction and see the oil derrick pumping around the clock — two economies in the same field.

october.oil_field: Full production. Ideal working weather. Tyler Rose Festival (established 1933) — the county's civic celebration combining the rose industry and community pride.
october.agricultural_remnant: Cotton picking at peak. Sweet potatoes, corn harvest.
october.social_civic: Tyler Rose Festival. County fairs. Court sessions.
october.economic: Strong for all sectors — oil, cotton, and rose industries at peak simultaneously.
october.weather_conditions: Cool and pleasant — 58-68F. The best working weather of the year.
october.narrative_hook: October in Tyler was when the Rose Festival and the oil derricks and the cotton gin all ran at once — {ancestor_name} in a county where three economies operated simultaneously and the one {pronoun_subject} worked determined which calendar ruled.

november.oil_field: Full production. Cooler weather increased outdoor work efficiency.
november.agricultural_remnant: Late cotton picking. Hog killing. Hunting season.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving. Hunting — deer season in the pine woods was a major social and economic event.
november.economic: Year-end oil production accounting. Cotton settlements.
november.narrative_hook: November in East Texas: {ancestor_name} working the rig by day and hunting deer in the pine woods on the day off — because the oil field paid the bills but the pine woods still fed the soul.

december.oil_field: Production continued through Christmas. Some companies gave Christmas Day off; others did not. The oil field ran on the calendar of the market, not the church.
december.social_civic: Christmas. Oil field families had more cash for Christmas than farm families had ever seen. The first generation of oil field Christmases were remembered as transformative — store-bought presents, a real tree, meat that was not fatback.
december.economic: Year-end. Oil field wages had fundamentally changed the economic calendar — a family that had lived on once-a-year cotton income now had weekly wages year-round.
december.narrative_hook: The first Christmas with oil field wages was one {ancestor_name}'s family remembered — store-bought presents, a ham that was not from the smokehouse, and the knowledge that the paycheck would come again next Friday regardless of what the weather or the cotton did.

narrative_hooks: The cotton calendar ruled East Texas for seventy years — {ancestor_name} lived by planting and picking, and the lay-by in July was the one week the crop let go.
The lumber industry added a winter calendar on top of the cotton calendar — {ancestor_name} logged when the sap was down and farmed when it rose, rarely idle and never rich.
The oil field erased the seasons — {ancestor_name} worked the same shift in July and January, and the paycheck arrived every week regardless of what the cotton or the weather did.
The Tyler Rose Festival in October was when {ancestor_name}'s county celebrated the one crop that was beautiful — roses blooming in a landscape of cotton and oil derricks.
Revival season in the pine grove was the spiritual harvest — {ancestor_name} under the brush arbor in August, hearing something the cotton field and the oil rig could not provide.
October was when everything peaked at once — cotton at the gin, oil at the wellhead, roses at the festival — and {ancestor_name} in {county} County had to decide which calendar to answer.
source_references: Campbell, Randolph B. An Empire for Slavery: The Peculiar Institution in Texas, 1821-1865. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 1989.
Maxwell, Robert S., and Robert D. Baker. Sawdust Empire: The Texas Lumber Industry, 1830-1940. College Station: Texas A&M University Press, 1983.
Olien, Roger M., and Diana Davids Olien. Oil in Texas: The Gusher Age, 1895-1945. Austin: University of Texas Press, 2002.
Sitton, Thad. Backwoodsmen: Stockmen and Hunters along a Big Thicket River Valley. Norman: University of Oklahoma Press, 1995.
Lowe, Richard G., and Randolph B. Campbell. Planters and Plain Folk: Agriculture in Antebellum Texas. Dallas: Southern Methodist University Press, 1987.
McDonald, Archie P., ed. Nacogdoches: Wilderness Outpost to Garden Spot of East Texas. Austin: Eakin Press, 2001.
